relevant_files.role: Core security scanner that orchestrates pattern matching and diff/file scanning
Pattern engine using regex-based detection with embedded JSON patterns
Core types for security findings, severity, confidence, and pattern definitions
Context-aware detection logic to determine when security scans should run
Embedded JSON database of 14 regex patterns for vulnerability detection across multiple languages
Performance benchmarks validating <10ms scan time for 500-line code samples

conventions.commits: Conventional commits with signed-off, DCO enforced via REUSE.toml and GitHub branch protection
conventions.testing: Comprehensive unit tests for scanner, patterns, types, and detection; integration tests in tests/ directory; criterion benchmarks for performance validation
conventions.linting: clippy with pedantic lints enabled, rustfmt for formatting, deny.toml for security linting, workspace lints in Cargo.toml
conventions.error_handling: thiserror and anyhow for error types, tracing for logging, backon for retry logic

patterns: Lazy static PatternEngine with embedded patterns.json for zero-config initialization
Hybrid regex-based scanning with file extension filtering and path-based ignore rules
Context-aware scan triggering based on labels, description keywords, and file paths
SARIF output generation for standardized vulnerability reporting

related_issues.title: feat(security): evaluate tree-sitter for AST-based vulnerability detection
related_issues.relevance: Primary issue scoping the tree-sitter evaluation with estimated 500-800 LOC, 14 patterns to convert, and hybrid architecture recommendation

related_issues.title: test(security): add negative test documenting multi-line detection limitation
related_issues.relevance: Documents the known limitation that regex-based scanner cannot detect multi-line vulnerabilities where source and sink are on different lines

constraints: Current regex-based scanner operates line-by-line, limiting detection to single-line patterns
Adding tree-sitter requires language-specific grammar crates (tree-sitter-rust, tree-sitter-python, etc.) which increase binary size
Tree-sitter queries use Scheme-like syntax requiring learning curve
Data-flow/taint tracking still requires custom logic beyond AST parsing
Maintainer preference for hybrid approach (regex for simple patterns, tree-sitter for multi-line/data-flow)
test_coverage: Comprehensive unit tests for scanner (10 tests), patterns (8 tests), types (5 tests), detection (9 tests), and validator. Benchmarks validate <10ms performance for 500-line samples. Tests cover: hardcoded secrets, SQL injection, path traversal, weak crypto, file extension filtering, false positives, line number accuracy, diff scanning, path ignoring, and multi-file scans. Validator tests LLM-based finding validation.

library_findings.relevant_api: Parser::set_language(), Query::new(), QueryCursor::matches(), capture names and node traversal APIs
library_findings.notes: Tree-sitter provides incremental parsing, language-aware syntax trees, and query system for pattern matching. Rust API is mature with 575+ code examples in Context7. Query syntax allows capturing nodes like (assignment_expression left: (identifier) @name) for precise pattern matching beyond regex line-by-line limitations.

library_findings.relevant_api: tree_sitter_rust::LANGUAGE constant, parser configuration, node type traversal, field access (e.g., child_by_field_name("name"))
library_findings.notes: Rust grammar for tree-sitter enables parsing Rust code into syntax trees. API examples show parsing, accessing function names, struct fields, and incremental editing. Provides node types and syntax highlighting queries.

approaches.name: Hybrid Regex + Tree-Sitter Scanner
approaches.description: Extend SecurityScanner to support tree-sitter-based scanning alongside existing regex patterns. Use regex for simple single-line patterns (hardcoded secrets, weak crypto) and tree-sitter for multi-line/data-flow patterns (SQL injection, command injection, XSS). Implement a new AstPatternEngine that parses files with appropriate tree-sitter grammar, executes tree-sitter queries, and performs data-flow analysis for taint tracking.
pros: Minimal architectural disruption - keeps existing regex patterns and scanner interface
Gradual migration path - can start with one language/pattern
Better detection of multi-line vulnerabilities (source on one line, sink on another)
Supports incremental parsing for fast re-scans on edits
Reuses existing ignore patterns, SARIF output, and validation logic
Maintains performance for simple patterns with regex
cons: Increased binary size from grammar crates (tree-sitter-rust, tree-sitter-python, etc.)
Added complexity in scanner orchestration (which engine to use for which pattern/file)
Learning curve for tree-sitter query syntax
Initial spike required to validate approach

approaches.name: Full Migration to Tree-Sitter
approaches.description: Replace regex-based PatternEngine entirely with tree-sitter-based detection. Convert all 14 patterns to tree-sitter queries, implement data-flow analysis for taint tracking, and build a new AstSecurityScanner. This would be a complete rewrite of the security scanning subsystem.
pros: More accurate vulnerability detection with full AST and data-flow analysis
Better maintainability as patterns are query-based rather than regex-based
Supports advanced analysis like control-flow and data-flow tracking
Future-proof for adding new vulnerability types
Potential performance benefits from incremental parsing
cons: Massive architectural change - nearly all security code rewritten
High risk and effort (500-800+ LOC, pattern conversion, testing)
Requires adding 5-10 language grammars in dependencies
Breaking change for scanner API consumers
Long migration timeline with potential regressions

approaches.name: Tree-Sitter Spike for Multi-Line SQL Injection
approaches.description: Create a focused spike: add tree-sitter-rust to dependencies, implement tree-sitter query for SQL injection pattern (source: user input variable, sink: execute() call on different lines), integrate with existing scanner via feature flag, and validate detection improvement over regex. Document findings and decide next steps.
pros: Low-risk experiment to validate tree-sitter benefits
Minimal code changes (add dependency, new module, feature flag)
Clear success criteria (detect multi-line SQL injection)
Quick feedback loop for architectural decision
Can be discarded if not valuable
cons: Spike may not represent production complexity
Limited scope may miss integration challenges
Not a complete solution - just validates feasibility

recommendation: Implement the **Hybrid Regex + Tree-Sitter Scanner** approach. This balances innovation with risk management by extending the existing architecture rather than replacing it. The hybrid approach allows leveraging tree-sitter's strengths for multi-line/data-flow patterns (SQL injection, command injection, XSS) while preserving regex efficiency for simple patterns. It aligns with the issue description's suggestion for a hybrid approach and maintains backward compatibility. Start with a spike implementing tree-sitter-rust for Rust files and SQL injection pattern, then incrementally add more languages and patterns based on validation results.
